summary: We looked at Durham, North Carolina, as an example when considering consolidation efforts back in the early 1960s. Durham had attempted consolidation before, but it was abortive at that time. Dr. Rankin from Duke University was heavily involved in Durham's effort, which we studied to understand what worked and what didn’t. Their experience was somewhat disheartening then, but it informed our approach.

Duke University played a role in our study of consolidation efforts through Dr. Rankin, who was part of the political science department there. He was deeply involved in Durham's consolidation attempt, which we examined around 1963 or 1964. His work helped provide insight into the challenges and possibilities of merging city and county governments. Duke’s academic perspective was valuable in framing our own efforts.

Around 1963 or 1964, we also looked at Jacksonville, Florida, as part of our review of consolidation efforts. Jacksonville was one of the places where consolidation had been tried, and we considered their status and results during our study. Their experience, along with Nashville and Durham, helped us understand the broader national context of city-county consolidation.

Nashville, Tennessee, was another city we examined around 1963 or 1964 when reviewing consolidation attempts. Like Jacksonville and Durham, Nashville’s experience provided us with examples of what had been tried and what challenges existed. This helped shape our thinking about the feasibility and methods of consolidation in our own community.

Stan Brookshire was the mayor at the time when consolidation became a major focus in the late 1960s. He was one of the key driving forces behind the effort, working alongside others like Charlie Lowe. Brookshire’s leadership was instrumental in moving the consolidation agenda forward, especially as the Chamber of Commerce made it a priority around 1967 or 1968.

Charlie Lowe was chairman of the Board of County Commissioners during the consolidation push in the late 1960s. He worked closely with Mayor Stan Brookshire and others to advance the cause. Lowe’s role was significant in appointing members to the Charter Commission and in shaping the political will behind consolidation.
cues: chairman of Board of County Commissioners

summary: The Chamber of Commerce was central to the consolidation efforts, especially from about 1967 or 1968 when it made consolidation part of its program of work. Charlie Crawford, a key figure at the Chamber, was a quiet but powerful force behind the scenes, persuading leaders like Brookshire and Lowe. The Chamber established committees, helped get legislation passed, and pushed the issue forward as a prime project.

Charlie Crawford was a behind-the-scenes powerhouse at the Chamber of Commerce during the consolidation movement. He was probably the single most influential person in making consolidation a prime Chamber project. Crawford worked hard to persuade key leaders such as Stan Brookshire and Charlie Lowe to support and advance the effort in the late 1960s.

The Charter Commission was appointed by Mayor Brookshire and Chairman Lowe to draft the consolidation plan. They chose to have broad representation from all segments of the community rather than a small committee of businessmen. I was part of a large advisory group that worked closely with the Commission, attending many meetings and reviewing drafts with help from the Institute of Government. The Commission’s work introduced many drastic changes, which ultimately contributed to the consolidation proposal’s defeat at the polls.

The Institute of Government assisted the Charter Commission by helping draft many versions of the consolidation plan. Their expertise was valuable during the late 1960s as we worked through complex governmental restructuring. I personally read many drafts late into the night, and the Institute’s input was crucial in shaping the proposals, even though the final plan was ultimately too complex for voters to accept.
